skill.name: analytics-tracking
skill.description: Helps teams plan, implement, test, and audit analytics tracking for websites, products, campaigns, and conversion funnels.
skill.summary: Create practical tracking plans, GA4 events, GTM guidance, UTM conventions, and validation checklists.
skill.author: coreyhaines31

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported high-risk patterns, but review found they are markdown tables, inline examples, documentation text, and one benign Google Analytics support URL. No executable files, prompt injection attempts, credential handling, or data exfiltration logic were found. The remaining concern is low privacy risk because the skill discusses analytics identifiers and external tracking tools.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings are markdown fences, tracking-plan tables, naming examples, JavaScript analytics snippets, and an output template. They are not instructions to execute shell commands and no command invocation logic is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are rendered markdown or client-side analytics examples, not executable skill code. No file in the analyzed structure contains scripts or shell command wrappers.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URL Is Benign
low_findings.description: The only hardcoded URL points users to official Google Analytics documentation for recommended events. It does not receive user data and is not used by executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears in explanatory documentation and there is no network request implementation. The surrounding section recommends using platform documentation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to cryptographic functions, hashing APIs, encryption code, or stored secrets. The affected lines are skill metadata, role guidance, naming guidance, and markdown tables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic code at the cited lines. The static labels appear to be triggered by unrelated words or markdown syntax.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance lines describe analytics planning concepts such as vanity metrics, identifiers, product fields, UTM terms, and table examples. They do not collect host information, enumerate files, inspect processes, or profile the runtime environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is marketing analytics data design, not system inspection. There are no commands or APIs that query local system state.

low_findings.title: Analytics Identifier Guidance Requires Privacy Review
low_findings.description: The skill suggests considering user_id and account_id properties while also warning against PII leakage. This is legitimate analytics guidance, but teams should validate consent, minimization, retention, and deletion requirements before implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill clearly discusses user identifiers and privacy safeguards. This is not malicious, but misuse could create compliance or privacy exposure.

content.value_statement: Teams often collect inconsistent analytics data that cannot answer business questions. This skill guides tracking plans, GA4 events, GTM setup, UTM naming, and validation steps.
actual_capabilities: Creates structured tracking plans for marketing sites, products, apps, and e-commerce funnels.
Recommends event names, event categories, properties, triggers, and conversion definitions.
Provides GA4 and Google Tag Manager implementation guidance with validation steps.
Defines UTM parameter conventions for campaign attribution and team documentation.
Identifies common analytics issues such as duplicate events, missing triggers, and wrong property values.
Includes privacy checkpoints for consent, data minimization, PII avoidance, and retention settings.
limitations: Does not connect to GA4, GTM, Mixpanel, Amplitude, or advertising platforms directly.
Does not verify live event traffic unless the user provides tool output or screenshots.
Requires business goals and implementation context to produce a useful tracking plan.
Does not replace legal review for privacy, consent, or regional compliance requirements.

use_cases.title: Launch Campaign Tracking
use_cases.description: Build consistent UTM rules and conversion events before a new acquisition campaign starts.

use_cases.title: Audit Product Analytics
use_cases.description: Review tracked product events and identify gaps that block funnel, onboarding, or retention analysis.

use_cases.title: Prepare GTM Implementation
use_cases.description: Translate measurement goals into tags, triggers, variables, data layer events, and test steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Tracking Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a website or product.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a tracking plan for my website. The main goal is [goal]. The key actions are [actions]. We use [analytics tools].

prompt_templates.title: Map Events to Business Questions
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate planning when goals are clear but events are not defined.
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn these business questions into analytics events, properties, and triggers: [questions]. Include GA4 conversion recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Existing Analytics
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of a current implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this analytics setup for gaps, duplicates, naming issues, privacy risks, and validation steps: [current setup details].

prompt_templates.title: Design a Full Measurement System
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced cross-functional planning for marketing, product, and engineering teams.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a complete measurement system for [business model]. Include event taxonomy, GTM structure, UTM conventions, conversion rules, privacy controls, and QA workflow.

output_examples.input: Plan tracking for a SaaS signup funnel.
output: A funnel event list covering signup started, account created, onboarding step completed, trial started, and subscription started.
Recommended properties such as signup method, plan type, onboarding step, campaign source, and user type.
A validation checklist for GA4 DebugView, GTM Preview Mode, duplicate event checks, and PII review.

output_examples.input: Improve our UTM naming for paid and email campaigns.
output: A naming convention for source, medium, campaign, content, and term values.
Examples for paid search, lifecycle email, partner referrals, and social campaigns.
Governance guidance for owners, dates, documentation, and review before launch.

output_examples.input: Audit our analytics after a redesign.
output: A prioritized list of likely issues, including missing triggers, changed button labels, duplicate tags, and broken form events.
A testing workflow for desktop, mobile, conversion pages, and logged-in user paths.
Recommendations for consent checks, user identifier handling, and data retention settings.

best_practices: Start with decisions and business questions before defining events.
Use consistent event and property names across tools and teams.
Validate tracking in debug tools before publishing tags or campaigns.
anti_patterns: Tracking every click without a clear decision or owner.
Sending names, emails, or other personal data into analytics properties.
Changing event names without documenting migration effects on reports.

faq.question: Can this skill implement tracking directly?
faq.answer: It provides plans, snippets, checklists, and implementation guidance. A developer or tag manager user still applies the changes.

faq.question: Does it support GA4?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes GA4 event planning, conversion setup, custom definitions, DebugView validation, and recommended event guidance.

faq.question: Does it support Google Tag Manager?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers tags, triggers, variables, data layer patterns, preview testing, folders, workspaces, and publish notes.

faq.question: Can it help with privacy requirements?
faq.answer: It includes consent, minimization, PII avoidance, retention, and deletion checkpoints. Legal review is still required for compliance decisions.

faq.question: Can it audit an existing setup?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide current events, tags, reports, screenshots, or observed issues, and it can identify gaps and validation steps.

faq.question: What information should users provide first?
faq.answer: Provide business goals, key conversions, current tools, implementation ownership, privacy requirements, and what is already tracked.
